steps.title: Identify what the experiment proves
steps.content: The passage describes an experiment: participants who touched a mug and toy were willing to pay much more for the items than those who were not allowed to touch them. The blank asks what this finding suggests — look for a conclusion that follows directly from that result, without going beyond it.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘people who mainly shop online probably spend more money every month than people who mainly shop in person do.’: The passage never discusses total monthly spending or compares overall budgets. In fact, the experiment suggests the opposite — touching raises willingness to pay, so online shoppers (who can't touch) would likely pay less. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘in-person shopping may make products seem more valuable than they seem if only viewed online.’: The experiment directly shows that physically touching a product increases perceived value. In-person shopping enables touch; online shopping does not. This conclusion follows logically from the evidence. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘retailers with in-person and online stores should charge the same price for a given product in both places.’: The passage makes no claim about what retailers "should" do. The experiment actually implies in-person stores could charge more, not that pricing must be equal. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘online retailers may be able to raise the prices they charge for products that are only available online.’: Products available only online still can't be touched, so the experiment gives no reason their prices could rise. This goes beyond and contradicts the evidence. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
